description:
You are working as a compliance analyst who must create a simple, self-contained audit trail for an existing Git project that already contains a sub-module.  
    Your manager wants a **single, plain-text log file** that captures the *exact* console output of the three commands listed below, executed from inside the repository’s root directory **/home/user/project**.

    Commands to capture (in this order):  
      1. `git status --short`  
      2. `git submodule status`  
      3. `git rev-parse HEAD`  

    Output file details  
      • Path: **/home/user/audit/git_audit_log.txt** (create the audit directory if it does not exist).  
      • Encoding: UTF-8.  
      • The file must follow the *precise* four-section template shown below.  
      • Section headers start with two equals signs `==`, followed by a single space, the command, another single space, and a final pair of equals signs.  
      • After every header line, reproduce the *verbatim* command output exactly as it appeared in your terminal session.  
      • There is **one blank line** between sections, and the file ends with a trailing newline.

    Template the automated tests will check (angle brackets show where your captured output goes, do not include them literally):

        == git status --short ==
        <verbatim output of git status --short>

        == git submodule status ==
        <verbatim output of git submodule status>

        == git rev-parse HEAD ==
        <verbatim output of git rev-parse HEAD>

    Additional guidance  
      • You do not need to modify, commit, or update anything in the repository; just run the commands and capture their outputs in the required format.  
      • Use only standard shell redirection or utilities that come pre-installed in a typical minimal Linux environment (e.g., `echo`, `printf`, `tee`, etc.).  
      • Do **not** hard-code the expected hashes; record exactly what the commands print in your current session.  
      • Make sure the final file location and content can be verified automatically, so double-check path, spacing, blank lines, and the trailing newline.

    When you are finished, `/home/user/audit/git_audit_log.txt` should exist and match the template above, containing the real command outputs from your environment.
truth:
Initial repository layout and state (before the agent starts):

    1. Main repository  
       Path: /home/user/project  
       • It is a valid Git working tree.  
       • HEAD commit hash: 7890abcd1234567890abcdef1234567890abcd  
       • No modified, untracked, or staged files.  
       • Tracked files:
         ├── README.md  (simple text file, content: "Main project\n")
         └── .gitmodules (content registers a single submodule at `libs/json`)

    2. Sub-module repository  
       Path (relative to main repo): /home/user/project/libs/json  
       • Commit checked out: abcd1234567890abcdef1234567890abcdef12  
       • Branch pointer: heads/master  
       • Clean working tree (no changes).

    Because the working tree is clean, `git status --short` outputs *nothing* (it prints an empty line or no lines, depending on Git version; both are considered empty output for grading).  
    `git submodule status` prints exactly:  
       " abcd1234567890abcdef1234567890abcdef12 libs/json (heads/master)"  
       (leading space is significant).  

    The automated test will verify that `/home/user/audit/git_audit_log.txt` exists and matches **exactly** the following content (with a trailing newline at EOF):

        == git status --short ==

        == git submodule status ==
         abcd1234567890abcdef1234567890abcdef12 libs/json (heads/master)

        == git rev-parse HEAD ==
        7890abcd1234567890abcdef1234567890abcd

    Notes for the checker:  
      • Accept either a single empty line **or no line at all** after the first header, because Git might emit no output if the working tree is clean.  
      • The important parts are header lines, correct blank-line placement between sections, the submodule status line (with leading space), the correct 40-character hashes, and the final newline.  
      • The agent has no root permissions; both `/home/user` and `/home/user/audit` are writable.
